title: Eoran
html:
The stairs are steep enough that you have to angle your weight forward on the descent and the stone under your boots is worn mirror-smooth from people doing exactly this before us. The platform is 6 stories above street level. Enough height to see the roofline of the first tier of buildings spreading out below, the city packed tighter than it looks from above.

Se-ah leads. I'm behind her, then Soo-Yun, Jae-won, Mi-rae. The eagle country man and the panda country woman follow without being invited, which I don't stop. The rest of the 61 are coming down in whatever order they sorted themselves into on the platform.

The staircase cuts through the platform's southern face with a low stone wall on the open side. Through the gaps in the wall, the city is close enough to read. The buildings on the first tier are built from the same stone as the platform: pale grey, dense, mortared with something dark between the courses. The architecture isn't any single thing I can place. It's been built by whoever got here first and extended by whoever came after, and the seams between the styles are visible in the walls if you look for them.

A woman is standing at the base of the stairs.

She's alone, leaning against the wall at the staircase's foot with her arms folded and her weight on one leg, watching us come down. She's in her early thirties, wearing a layered set of clothing that reads as practical: a dark jacket with the cuffs rolled, trousers with enough pockets to suggest she uses them, boots with real wear on the soles. A short blade at her hip, sheathed and latched. She's been waiting long enough to settle into the lean.

Se-ah slows without stopping. I come down beside her.

"Cycle 7?" the woman asks. Her accent has the flat vowels of somewhere in maple country.

"That's what the angel said," I tell her.

She nods once, reading the column behind us. Her eyes pause on the machete in my hand, then on Soo-Yun, then move on.

"How many?"

"61."

Something crosses her face that isn't quite surprise. "That's high. Cycle 5 put out 38."

"The kappa seeding was light this cycle," Se-ah says.

The woman looks at her. "You got a briefing?"

"The angel mentioned it before she left."

"Figures." The woman pushes off the wall and straightens. She's a few centimeters shorter than Se-ah and a head shorter than Jae-won but the lean didn't read as relaxed — it read as efficient. "My name's Caitlin Ward. I've been in Eoran since Cycle 2. I'm not here to rob you."

"That's a specific thing to open with," I say.

"Cycle 3 arrivals got robbed at the staircase foot inside the first 10 minutes," she says. "By Cycle 2 people. Felt worth mentioning." She tilts her head toward the street behind her. "You want to know what you're walking into or do you want to figure it out yourselves?"

Se-ah looks at me. I look at Caitlin.

"Talk while we move," I say.

The street at the base of the platform is wide enough for 3 carts abreast. Stone-paved, with channels cut at the edge for water drainage, and a smell of woodsmoke and food underneath something older that belongs to the stone itself. People move through it without looking at us: men and women in various states of Eoran-practical clothing, a few with visible weapons, most without. They're not ignoring us. They're choosing not to stare, which is different.

"There are 4 factions with real territory in Eoran right now," Caitlin says, walking at a pace that covers ground without signaling urgency. "The Iron Table controls the market district and the southern gate. Cycle 1 and 2 core. They don't recruit, they buy. If you have something they want and you're not competition, they'll trade fairly."

"And if you are competition?"

"They don't rush it. They wait until you're in debt, or hungry, or separated. Then they make an offer."

I file that.

"Second faction is called the Covenant. Cycle 3 origin, religious structure, runs the northern residential tier. They recruit aggressively from new arrivals. They'll be at the platform within the hour with food and an offer."

"What's the offer?"

"Protection and housing, in exchange for 30% of everything you earn and mandatory attendance at their assemblies."

Jae-won makes a sound. Caitlin doesn't look at him.

"Third is the Freerunners. No fixed territory, no structure, mostly Cycle 4 and 5 stragglers who didn't want to join the first two. They're scattered through the eastern quarter. Useful for information, bad for backup when it matters."

"And the fourth?" Se-ah asks.

Caitlin pauses half a step before answering. "The Red Ledger. They control the gate passage to the outer zones. If you want to leave the city to hunt or gather or find the dungeon entries, you pay a toll. Cycle 1 founded it. The man who runs it has been here since the first drop and he hasn't gotten softer."

"Who are you with?" I ask.

"Was Freerunners. Currently independent." She says it without defense. "I came down here because 61 new people arriving without a briefing is bad for everyone. Panicked newcomers make bad decisions that become other people's problems."

That's probably true. It's also a reason to make herself useful to 61 people on day one, which she has to know we're calculating.

"What do you want from this?" I ask.

She looks at me sideways. "I want to not be the only Cycle 2 person who is still unaffiliated by Cycle 8." She stops at the corner of the street and turns to face the group trailing behind us. 61 people have funneled into the street and are moving in an uneven column, reading everything around them. A few are already peeling off toward storefronts. She raises her voice just enough to carry. "Tell them to stay together for now. The Iron Table has people watching the platform street. Stragglers get approached."

I pull the skill menu while Caitlin talks to the wider group. It surfaces in my vision the way all the system screens do: clear, steady, waiting.

---

Available Skill Points: 6

Class: Scavenger

Available Skills:

[Salvage I] — Identify usable components in found or discarded items. Passive.

[Patch I] — Restore minor functionality to damaged equipment using available materials. Active. Cost: 10 HP.

[Light Step I] — Reduce sound generation during movement by 40%. Passive.

[Threat Read I] — Passive perception increase vs. hostile entities within 15m. +3 Perception vs. threats.

Each skill costs 2 points.

---

I take Threat Read I and Light Step I. That spends all 6. Threat Read is the only thing that changes what I can do right now, and Light Step costs nothing to run. Salvage and Patch are useful once I have materials and time, which I don't have yet.

---

Skills Acquired: [Threat Read I], [Light Step I]

Skill Points Remaining: 0

---

The Covenant people Caitlin predicted don't arrive at the platform first.

Two men in matching dark green come up the platform street from the south end while Caitlin is still speaking to the group. They're moving at the specific pace of people who aren't hurrying because they don't need to. The one on the left is carrying a ledger under his arm. The one on the right has a hand resting near a blade that's nicer than anything I took off a kappa.

My Threat Read lights them up at 14 meters: a faint pressure in the Perception channel, not aggression, more like attention. They're reading the group the same way I read the kappa position in the forest.

Caitlin clocks them without turning around. "Iron Table. Two minutes earlier than I expected."

"Are they a problem?" Jae-won asks.

"Not immediately," she says. "They want to know who the organizer is. They'll make contact with whoever looks like they're running things and skip everyone else."

She glances at me.

I look at Se-ah.

Se-ah is already watching the two men with an expression that says she's been waiting for them to arrive and has had time to decide what to do about it.

"Se-ah," I say.

She steps forward before I finish saying her name.

She meets the two Iron Table men 10 meters from the main group, positioning herself so that the conversation happens away from the column's earshot. The man with the ledger opens it. Se-ah doesn't look at the ledger.

Mi-rae appears at my left shoulder. "Good choice," she says.

"She looked like she wanted it," I tell her.

Mi-rae watches Se-ah for a moment. "She's going to be a problem eventually."

"I know."

"You don't seem concerned."

"I know that too," I say.

The conversation between Se-ah and the Iron Table men runs about 3 minutes. The man with the ledger writes something and doesn't show her what. The one with the blade doesn't move his hand. They leave the way they came, back down the platform street toward the southern market district.

Se-ah comes back without changing pace.

"They wanted a headcount and class distribution," she says. "I gave them the headcount. I told them class assignment was still pending for most of the group."

"Is that true?" Soo-Yun asks.

"For most of the people on this street, yes," Se-ah says. "They touched the pillar. They got a class. But they haven't read the notification yet because they were too busy being terrified to focus on it."

That's accurate. A third of the 61 probably haven't processed half their tutorial notifications.

"What did they offer?" I ask.

"A meeting. Tomorrow, market district, no obligation." Se-ah looks down the street. "They're going to do the same math we are. 61 survivors is a large class. They want to know the distribution before they make a real offer."

Caitlin is watching Se-ah with the expression of someone revising an estimate upward.

"We need somewhere to sleep tonight," Jae-won says. He isn't wrong. It's the most practical problem on the board and nobody has addressed it.

"I know a place," Caitlin says. "Freerunner-adjacent, eastern quarter. 8 people max, more if you're comfortable sleeping in shifts."

61 people, 8 spaces, 1 night. The math produces a list of problems and I'm not going to solve all of them on the platform street before dark.

I look up at the platform above us. The last of the 61 are still coming down. The sun in Eoran is at a position that puts it somewhere past mid-afternoon. A few hours of light left, and somewhere in this city 2,400 people with a head start are going about their business with the knowledge that 61 new arrivals just dropped onto the platform.

'Should have slept more in the restaurant job.'

"Move," I tell the group. "Eastern quarter. Together."
